title: Tess of the d'Urbervilles
author: Thomas Hardy
overture: Tess of the d'Urbervilles walks. From the Vale of Blackmoor to the dairies of the Froom, up onto the starve-acre uplands and at last to Stonehenge, almost every mile is on foot, across a Dorset that Hardy renamed but barely disguised. Three lives cross and recross one county; the map plays them together, chapter by chapter.

sources.name: Thomas Hardy's map of 'The Wessex of the Novels' (1895)
F. B. Pinion, A Hardy Companion (1968)

regions.name: The valley of the Great Dairies

characters.name: Tess Durbeyfield
characters.role: Cottager's daughter and last of the real d'Urbervilles; nearly everything on this map she crosses on foot

characters.name: Angel Clare
characters.role: The vicar's freethinking son, learning farming at Talbothays; a year in Brazil, offstage, divides his two halves of this map

characters.name: Alec d'Urberville
characters.role: The sham d'Urberville of The Slopes — rake, then ranter, then rake again; he crosses Tess's path at long intervals, and Sandbourne is his last stop

locations.name: Marnhull, Dorset
locations.note: Marnhull, by universal identification since Hardy's own 1895 Wessex map and Hermann Lea (1913).
locations.story: Tess's birthplace in the Vale of Blackmoor: the May club-walking where a young Angel Clare, passing on a walking tour, dances — though not with her. She comes home to it in trouble twice, and her father's grand unpaid-for headstone ends up in its churchyard.
locations.quote: The village of Marlott lay amid the north-eastern undulations of the beautiful Vale of Blakemore, or Blackmoor

locations.name: Shaftesbury
locations.note: Shaftesbury, by universal identification (Hardy's Wessex map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The hill-town on the Vale's rim, shining in Tess's childhood evenings like a row of lamps. It is her gateway out of Blackmoor: the Chaseborough carrier's van starts from here.
locations.quote: above all, the town of Shaston standing majestically on its height; its windows shining like lamps in the evening sun

locations.name: Pentridge, Dorset
locations.note: Trantridge is universally identified with Pentridge (Hardy's map; Lea 1913). The Slopes itself — the Stoke-d'Urbervilles' bright new villa — is Hardy's invention, so the house is folded into the village here.
locations.story: The Slopes, the spick-and-span house of the Stoke-d'Urbervilles: northern money wearing a bought name. Tess comes to claim kin with people who are no kin at all, keeps Mrs d'Urberville's poultry, and is taught to whistle to bullfinches under Alec's supervision.
locations.quote: She alighted from the van at Trantridge Cross, and ascended on foot a hill in the direction of the district known as The Chase

locations.name: Cranborne
locations.note: Cranborne, identified since Lea (1913) — Hardy drew the novel's inn from the real Fleur-de-Lys at Cranborne.
locations.story: The decayed market-town where the Trantridge work-folk dance and drink away their Saturday nights. On a September fair-night Tess waits too long for company on the walk home, and accepts a lift from the wrong man.
locations.quote: going every Saturday night, when work was done, to Chaseborough, a decayed market-town two or three miles distant

locations.name: Cranborne Chase
locations.note: Cranborne Chase, named all but openly; the identification is universal (Hardy's map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The oldest wood in England — primaeval yews and oaks, Druidical mistletoe. Riding home in fog, Alec loses the way here not quite honestly; what happens under the trees Hardy keeps in darkness, and it is the night that breaks Tess's life in two.
locations.quote: A bit of The Chase—the oldest wood in England.

locations.name: The Frome valley, near West Stafford
locations.note: The dairy is invented: Lea (1913) treats it as a composite of Froom-valley dairies, the name borrowed from a Hardy family farm at West Stafford. The valley itself is certain; this point stands in the water-meadows south-east of Dorchester.
locations.story: Dairyman Crick's great dairy in the Valley of the Great Dairies, where Tess rallies. The heart of the book's one happy stretch: the dawn milkings, the garden at dusk, and Angel Clare — by harvest she has agreed, against her own conscience, to marry him.
locations.quote: she found herself on a summit commanding the long-sought-for vale, the Valley of the Great Dairies

locations.name: Woolbridge Manor, Wool
locations.note: Woolbridge Manor at Wool, by universal identification (Hardy's map; Lea 1913) — and a gift of a fact: the manor really belonged to the Turbervilles, the family whose decayed name Hardy borrowed.
locations.story: The honeymoon house, once a d'Urberville manor: the wedding-night confessions, the dreadful ancestral portraits on the stair, and Angel's sleepwalk with Tess in his arms to a stone coffin in the abbey ruins. The marriage lasts three days here.
locations.quote: once portion of a fine manorial residence, and the property and seat of a d'Urberville, but since its partial demolition a farmhouse

locations.name: Bere Regis

image.caption: Bere Regis — Hardy's Kingsbere — in a watercolour of 1910
image.credit: E. W. Haslehust, from The Heart of Wessex, 1910 · public domain

locations.note: Bere Regis — the real Turberville vaults and window are in the parish church, exactly as the novel describes (Hardy's map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The ancestral parish, with rows of d'Urbervilles in the church vaults. Evicted from Marlott on Old Lady-Day, the family finds its promised rooms already let and sets up the four-post bedstead against the church wall, beneath the d'Urberville Window.
locations.quote: Hard by, the aisle of the church called the d'Urberville Aisle looked on imperturbably.

locations.name: Dorchester
locations.note: Dorchester, the capital of Hardy's Wessex (his map; Lea 1913). Its part in this novel is a passing one.
locations.story: The county town at the edge of Tess's map: it is on the dark road to its Saturday market, with the beehives, that the mail-cart spears Prince the horse — the small disaster that sets the whole story moving.
locations.quote: if they were to be delivered to the retailers in Casterbridge before the Saturday market began

locations.name: Beaminster
locations.note: Beaminster, by universal identification (Hardy's map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The Clare vicarage, Angel's home. Tess walks fifteen miles here in midwinter to ask his parents for help, and turns back at the gate — her nerve finished off by his brothers' talk on the road, and by the confiscation of her hidden walking-boots.
locations.quote: about noon she paused by a gate on the edge of the basin in which Emminster and its Vicarage lay

locations.name: Evershot
locations.note: Evershot, by universal identification (Lea 1913); the novel's Sow-and-Acorn is the real Acorn Inn.
locations.story: The halfway village on Tess's great midwinter walk: breakfast at a cottage by the church going out, a sadder halt coming back — and, a little beyond, the barn where the voice of the preaching ranter is one she knows.
locations.quote: She made a halt here, and breakfasted a second time, heartily enough—not at the Sow-and-Acorn, for she avoided inns, but at a cottage by the church

locations.name: Near Plush, Dorset
locations.note: The farm is invented and the site disputed: Lea (1913) placed it near Nettlecombe Tout, Kay-Robinson (1972) argued for the Doles Ash area above Plush. This point stands on that stretch of chalk upland.
locations.story: The starve-acre farm: swede-hacking in the rain, reed-drawing in snow, the relentless threshing-machine, all under a farmer with a grudge. Alec d'Urberville — newly converted, then swiftly unconverted — lays siege to her here through the winter.
locations.quote: There was no exaggeration in Marian's definition of Flintcomb-Ash farm as a starve-acre place.

locations.name: Bournemouth
locations.note: Bournemouth, by universal identification (Hardy's map; Lea 1913). The Herons, the boarding-house where Tess is found, is invented, and is folded into the town here.
locations.story: The glittering new pleasure-city where Alec keeps Tess at The Herons, and where Angel finds her too late. Within the hour of his leaving, Alec lies dead in the upstairs room, and Tess is gone after her husband.
locations.quote: It was a city of detached mansions; a Mediterranean lounging-place on the English Channel

locations.name: The New Forest, Hampshire
locations.note: Scholarship has never settled Bramshurst Court: Lea offered no identification, and later guesses (Moyles Court near Ringwood is sometimes proposed) have never stuck. This point simply stands in the New Forest, on the fugitives' northward line.
locations.story: An empty furnished mansion with the blinds down and grass growing on the drive: five days of borrowed married happiness — the only ones Tess and Angel are given — until a caretaker's morning round sends them north again.
locations.quote: It is Bramshurst Court. You can see that it is shut up, and grass is growing on the drive.

locations.name: Salisbury
locations.note: Salisbury, by universal identification (Hardy's map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The fugitives pass through at midnight, keeping off the pavements so their footsteps will not echo, the cathedral rising dimly on their left hand — lost on them now.
locations.quote: The intercepting city, ancient Melchester, they were obliged to pass through in order to take advantage of the town bridge for crossing a large river

locations.name: Stonehenge
locations.story: The heathen temple on the open plain, met blind in the dark. Tess sleeps on a sun-warmed slab among the pillars while Angel keeps watch; the police close in with the dawn, and she is ready — 'It is as it should be.'
locations.quote: Older than the centuries; older than the d'Urbervilles!

locations.name: Winchester
locations.note: Winchester, by universal identification (Hardy's map); the prison on the city's western side still stands.
locations.story: The old capital of Wessex, where Tess is tried and hanged. Angel and 'Liza-Lu climb the great West Hill and watch, hand in hand, as the black flag moves slowly up the prison tower.
locations.quote: The city of Wintoncester, that fine old city, aforetime capital of Wessex, lay amidst its convex and concave downlands in all the brightness and warmth of a July morning

movements.note: Sent by her mother to claim kin with the rich d'Urbervilles
The twice-weekly carrier's van; she alights at Trantridge Cross
Home the same day, Alec's roses at her breast
The last miles down into the Vale on foot
To live, as keeper of Mrs d'Urberville's fowls — Alec driving the last downhill stage at a deliberately reckless gallop
Hovering about the town on the fair-night, cigar alight
The September night when fair and market coincide; her comrades dance into the small hours
She rides pillion to escape a quarrel on the dark road; in the fog he loses the way — not quite honestly
Of the return the novel says nothing
The Sunday-morning walk home with her basket, a few weeks later; Alec overtakes her and drives her part of the way
Two years and more on: hired trap to Stourcastle, a farmer's spring-cart to Weatherbury, the last miles on foot over the heath
The wedding-day drive down the valley in the creaking old brougham, to a former d'Urberville house for the honeymoon
The parting, five days married: he sets her down within a walk of home, by Nuttlebury, and the road divides them
Three weeks after the wedding, to tell his parents of Brazil. His track pauses here: a year in Brazil, offstage, before he returns to Emminster fever-worn
One leg standing for a wandering summer and autumn of field-work as far west as Port-Bredy; the night among the dying pheasants falls on this road
The fifteen-mile midwinter walk to Angel's parents, past the stone at Cross-in-Hand; breakfast at a cottage by the church
By Benvill Lane, the walking-boots hidden in a hedge for the journey home
Her nerve gone at the vicarage gate — and the boots gone too, carried off for the deserving poor
Reappearing after the missing years as a ranting convert, mid-circuit, preaching in a barn on the Evershead road
Past the barn where the preacher is Alec d'Urberville; he follows, and at Cross-in-Hand makes her swear on the stone never to tempt him
Walking after Tess as far as Cross-in-Hand; through the late winter he besieges the farm
Summoned to her mother's sickbed: fifteen miles through Blackmoor by night
He follows — the firelit allotment plot, a pitchfork in his hand
Old Lady-Day: the family evicted, their goods on a hired waggon; the promised rooms are let, and the beds go up against the church wall
Still in pursuit: it is his form Tess finds stretched on the altar-tomb in the d'Urberville aisle
Offstage, between chapters: with her family destitute, she yields at last and goes to Alec at The Herons
His last remove; he dies at Sandbourne
Home from Brazil, searching: a hired trap along Benvill Lane and past Cross-in-Hand. At the farm no 'Mrs Clare' has ever been heard of
A borrowed gig to the Vale's edge, the last stretch on foot — to find strangers in the house and John Durbeyfield's headstone unpaid for
Hired beyond Shaston to Joan's new village for the address — 'She is at Sandbourne' — then the last train down
She catches him on the road out of town — 'I have done it—I don't know how' — and they walk inland until an empty furnished house takes them in
Five days' grace ended by a caretaker's morning round; they keep a bee-line north
Through the midnight streets, off the pavements, then the turnpike-road onto the open plain — and pillars rising out of the dark
Taken at dawn from the warm slab where she slept — 'I am ready'
To the great West Hill with 'Liza-Lu, one July morning, to watch the black flag go up
